content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 5, 1992


Timothy P. Hayes
McDonough, Holland & Allen
P.O. Box 776
422-A Century Park Drive
Yuba City, CA 95992-0776

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-385

Dear Mr. Hayes:

You have requested advice concerning application of the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act")¹ to the duties of Councilmembers Carl Malotte, Stanley
Nader and Charles Kellar and Planning Commissioners Marshall Weir
and Eva Benedict.

QUESTION

May the above Councilmembers and Planning Commissioners
participate in any of the pending annexations in the City of
Lincoln?

CONCLUSION

The facts and conclusions vary as to each public official.
Please see the individual analysis and discussion for each
individual official at the end of this letter.

FACTS

The City of Lincoln will soon be acting upon several pending
annexation requests comprising approximately 3,000 acres, or 4.7
square miles. The property is currently in Placer County and
zoned agricultural. If annexed into the city, it is anticipated
that a variety of zoning uses will be implemented, consisting
mainly of single-family housing, but also including open space,
industrial and commercial. The pending annexations will affect

_______________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-91-385
Page 2


nearly 2.5 miles of the southerly and southeasterly boundaries of
the city, which is where the majority of the population resides.
Lincoln has a population of approximately 7,500 residents and
covers an area of approximately 6.8 square miles.

All of the principal residences of the city council members
and the planning commission members are located in the City of
Lincoln, and all their residences are more than 300 feet from any
pending or potential annexation applications.

Councilman Carl Malotte

Mr. Malotte, in addition to his principal residence, owns
greater than a 10% share in Malotte Manufacturing, Inc., which
manufactures fiberglass. His company has a month-to-month lease
on property located at 390 G Street, which is approximately 2,125
feet and 2,750 feet from two different pending annexations, and
approximately 3,000 feet from a potential annexation.

Councilman Stanley P. Nader

Mr. Nader, in addition to his principal residence, owns
greater than a 10% interest in a family farming operation which
consists of real property and a feed store which located
outside the city limits, and over three miles from the closest
pending or proposed annexation.

Councilman Charles A. Kellar

Mr. Kellar's principal residence is located on two lots at
420-7th Street, approximately 2,125 feet from a potential an-
nexation and 2,500 feet from a pending annexation. There is a
separate guest house on the property, which has never been rented.
Mr. Kellar's relatives live in the guest house.

Planning Commissioner Marshall Weir

In addition to his principal residence, Mr. Weir has the
following real property interests:

a) a deed of trust in a residence at 565 East Avenue and
ownership of a vacant parcel south of 565 East Avenue--both of
which are approximately 430 feet from a potential annexation and
1,875 feet and 5,000 feet from two different pending annexations.

b) ownership of a vacant parcel east of 565 East Avenue,
which is within 300 feet of a potential annexation, and 1,875 feet
and 5,000 feet from two different pending annexations.

c) ownership of vacant property at 121 Ina Way, which is 350
feet and 2,490 from two different pending annexations and 2,250
feet from a potential annexation.

Our File No. A-91-385
Page 3


d)  a deed of trust on a residence at 330 E Street, which is
approximately 1,375 feet and 2,750 feet from two different pending
annexations and 2,250 feet from a potential annexation.

Planning Commissioner Eva Benedict

In addition to her principal residence, Ms. Benedict owns:

a)  real properties at 510 and 512 D Street, consisting of
rentals which are approximately 1,750 feet from the nearest
pending annexation, and 4,000 feet and 5,000 feet from other
pending annexations.

b)  real property at 670 M Street, consisting of rentals,
which is approximately 3,750 feet from the nearest pending
annexation.

c)  rental property at 143 E. Fifth Street, which is approximately 270 feet from a potential annexation and 500 feet from
an actual pending annexation.

ANALYSIS

The Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in, or using his official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest.  (Section 87100.)  A public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his immediate family or on, among other
things:

(a)  Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1000) or more.

(b)  Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1000) or more.

(c)  Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

Our File No. A-91-385
Page 4


(d)  Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

Section 87103(a)-(d).

The elected councilmembers and planning commissioners of the
City of Lincoln are public officials (Section 82048) and must
disqualify themselves from any governmental decisions which will
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on--among
other things--their real property and business interests.

Foreseeability

The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not
required; however, an effect that is merely a possibility is not
reasonably foreseeable. (Downey Cares v. Downey Community
Development Com. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983; In re Thorner (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 198.)

Materiality

The Commission has adopted several regulations which define
material financial effect. Regulation 18702 sets forth the
general guidelines for determining whether an official's financial
interest in a decision is "material" as required by Section 87103.
If the official's financial interest is directly involved in the
decision, Regulation 18702.1 applies to determine materiality. If
the official's financial interest is indirectly affected by the
decision, or if the effect of the decision is not material under
Regulation 18702.1, Regulations 18702.2 through 18702.6 apply to
determine whether the effect of the decision is material.

Regulation 18702.3 contains guidelines for determining when
the effect of a decision is material as to real property which is
indirectly involved in a decision. Regulation 18702.3 states in
pertinent part:

(a)  The effect of a decision is material as
to real property in which an official has a direct,
indirect or beneficial ownership interest (not
including a leasehold interest), if any of the
following applies:

(1)  The real property in which the
official has an interest, or any part of that
real property, is located within a 300-foot
radius of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the

Our File No. A-91-385
Page 5


subject of the decision, unless the decision
will have no financial effect upon the
official's real property interest.

(2) The decision involves construction
of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer,
storm drainage or similar facilities, and the
real property in which the official has an
interest will receive new or substantially
improved services.

(3) The real property in which the
official has an interest is located outside a
radius of 300 feet and any part of the real
property is located within a radius of 2,500
feet of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of:

(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000)
or more on the fair market value of the
real property in which the official has
an interest; or

(B) Will affect the rental value of
the property by $1,000 or more per 12
month period.

* * *

(c) For decisions which may affect an
interest in real property but which do not involve
a subject property from which the distances
prescribed in subdivisions (a) and (b) can be
determined, the monetary standards contained in
subdivision (a)(3)(A) and (B) shall be applied.

Under Regulation 18702.3(d), there are a number of factors
which must be considered in determining whether a decision will
have a financial effect of $10,000 or more on the fair market
value or $1,000 or more per twelve month period on the rental
value. These factors include, but are not limited to:

(1) The proximity of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the magnitude of
the proposed project or change in use in
relationship to the property in which the official
has an interest;

Our File No. A-91-385
Page 6


(2) Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will affect the development potential
or income producing potential of the property;

(3) In addition to the foregoing, in the case
of residential property, whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will result in a
change to the character of the neighborhood
including, but not limited to, effects on traffic,
view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood.

(Emphasis added.)

Regulation 18702.2 defines when the effect of a decision is
material to a business entity indirectly involved in a decision.
This regulation is very fact specific and is based on dollar
threshold amounts of net tangible assets, pre-tax income, gross
revenues and expenses. Since we do not have any financial
information about the councilmembers' businesses, we do not know
which subdivision of Regulation 18702.2 applies to this situation.
Therefore, it is incumbent upon you to analyze this regulation
accordingly.

We do not have sufficient information to provide an answer as
to whether each public official may participate in the annexation
decisions. Therefore, we have provided the pertinent regulations
for you to analyze whether there will be a material financial
effect on the financial interests of each official.

Councilman Carl Malotte

Pursuant to Regulation 18702.2, if the annexation will have a
material financial effect on Mr. Malotte's business, he may not
participate in any decisions regarding the annexation.

Councilman Stanley P. Nader

Since Mr. Nader's property is located outside the city limits
and more than 3 miles from any pending annexations, it is
considered outside the jurisdiction of the city (Section 82035).
However, if his business has done business within the jurisdiction
in the past 2 years (Section 82034), the financial effect of the
decisions on his business must be analyzed under Regulation
18702.2.

Councilman Charles A. Kellar

Mr. Kellar's property interest must be analyzed under
Regulation 18702.3(a)(3) since his property is within 2,500 feet
of an annexation. If there will be a material financial effect on
his property, he may not participate.

Our File No. A-91-385
Page 7




Planning Commissioner Marshall Weir

Mr. Weir has several property interests which must be
analyzed under Regulation 18702.3. One property is within 300
feet of the annexation and several properties are within 300 to
2,500 feet of other annexations. The property within 300 feet
presents a disqualifying interest, unless the decision will have
no financial effect upon the property. If this is the case, the
financial effect on the other properties must be analyzed under
Regulation 18702.3(a)(3).

Planning Commissioner Eva Benedict

Ms. Benedict has a number of property interests which must
also be analyzed under Regulation 18702.3. One property is within
300 feet of an annexation, another property is within 2,500 feet
of an annexation, and a third property is beyond 2,500 feet of an
annexation. The property within 300 feet presents a disqualifying
interest, unless the decision will have no financial effect upon
the property. If so, the financial effect on the other properties
must be analyzed under Regulation 18702.3(a)(3) and (b).

Public Generally--Small Jurisdictions

Regulation 18703.1 (enclosed) presents an exception whereby the
effect of a governmental decision on the principal residence of a
public official is not distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally if certain conditions are met. From the facts
presented, it appears that all the conditions are met only by the
city councilmembers, with the exception of Mr. Kellar.

Please be advised that this exception applies only to the
effect of a decision on an official's principal residence. In the
case of all the public officials discussed above, each may have
other financial interests which may require disqualification.

Public Generally

Even when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision is material, disqualification is required
only if the effect of the decision on an official's economic
interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally. (Section 87100.) Thus, disqualification is required
unless the decision will affect a public official's economic
interests in substantially the same manner as it will affect a
significant segment of the public. (Regulation 18703.)

Our File No. A-91-385
Page 8


     I trust this letter has answered your questions.  If you have
any further questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        By:  Jill Stecher
                                             Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JS:aa

sections.analysis:
The Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in, or using his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) A public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official or a member of his immediate family or on, among other things:

(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth one thousand dollars ($1000) or more.

(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand dollars ($1000) or more.

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.

Section 87103(a)-(d).

The elected councilmembers and planning commissioners of the City of Lincoln are public officials (Section 82048) and must disqualify themselves from any governmental decisions which will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on—among other things—their real property and business interests.

Foreseeability

The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required; however, an effect that is merely a possibility is not reasonably foreseeable. (Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Com. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Materiality

The Commission has adopted several regulations which define material financial effect. Regulation 18702 sets forth the general guidelines for determining whether an official’s financial interest in a decision is "material" as required by Section 87103. If the official’s financial interest is directly involved in the decision, Regulation 18702.1 applies to determine materiality. If the official’s financial interest is indirectly affected by the decision, or if the effect of the decision is not material under Regulation 18702.1, Regulations 18702.2 through 18702.6 apply to determine whether the effect of the decision is material.

Regulation 18702.3 contains guidelines for determining when the effect of a decision is material as to real property which is indirectly involved in a decision. Regulation 18702.3 states in pertinent part:

(a) The effect of a decision is material as to real property in which an official has a direct, indirect or beneficial ownership interest (not including a leasehold interest), if any of the following applies:

(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of that real property, is located within a 300-foot radius of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is the

subject of the decision, unless the decision will have no financial effect upon the official's real property interest.

(2) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real property in which the official has an interest will receive new or substantially improved services.

(3) The real property in which the official has an interest is located outside a radius of 300 feet and any part of the real property is located within a radius of 2,500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the decision and the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of:

(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or more on the fair market value of the real property in which the official has an interest; or

(B) Will affect the rental value of the property by $1,000 or more per 12 month period.
